location.origin: Central District, Seattle, Washington. No birth date, death date, or biographical details have been located in any online music database, archive, compilation, historical article, or oral history source reviewed for this degree. The CSV data places English at coordinates 47.608, -122.2976 with the explicit neighborhood designation 'Central District' and the label designation 'local.'
location.formative: Central District, Seattle (1960s-1970s). The Central District's R&B and soul scene operated from approximately 1965 through 1975, with venues including the Black and Tan Club, the Golden Crown, the Trojan Horse, the District Tavern, and the Birdland Supper Club. KYAC (1250 AM), Seattle's Black-owned radio station (1965-1981), broadcast local soul and funk. Kearney Barton's Audio Recording studio captured many local acts. Larry English performed within this ecosystem. [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- specific venues, dates, and musical collaborations cannot be confirmed from available sources.]
location.career: No career details documented in any available source. The CSV designates English as an R&B musician active in the 1960s-1970s with E=5 energy and the 'local' label, indicating sustained, high-energy participation in the Central District's musical scene without a major label deal. The 'local' label is the first unnamed label designation in the engine (degrees 29-32 were Prestige, Atlantic, Mercury, Mercury). The documentation gap itself is the primary finding: Larry English represents the base of the Central District's musical pyramid -- the local musicians whose nightly work in the clubs created the conditions from which nationally famous musicians emerged, but whose own documentation did not survive.

labels: local (no named label identified; self-released 45s, local studio recordings, or unrecorded live performances -- the specific format of English's musical output cannot be determined from available sources)

associated_musicians.name: Central District R&B scene (collective)
associated_musicians.role: Scene participants -- the aggregate of musicians, vocalists, instrumentalists, and bandleaders who performed in Central District venues from the mid-1960s through the mid-1970s
associated_musicians.note: Larry English cannot be linked to specific musicians from available sources. However, the Central District scene was small enough (four-mile radius, documented venues, single radio station KYAC) that overlap between active musicians was structurally inevitable. English would have shared stages, audiences, and musical community with the documented musicians of the era: Dave Lewis, Patrinell Staten, Black on White Affair, Cold Bold & Together, Robbie Hill's Family Affair, Cookin' Bag, Soul Swingers, and others. [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- specific collaborations cannot be confirmed.]

associated_musicians.name: Kearney Barton (possible)
associated_musicians.note: Barton (1931-2012) was Seattle's most prolific recording engineer and captured much of the Central District's soul and funk output. If English recorded at all, Barton's Audio Recording studio is the most likely location. However, English does not appear on any known Barton-recorded release or in the Barton archive documentation. [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- Barton's complete client list is not publicly available.]

associated_musicians.name: KYAC radio (possible)
associated_musicians.note: KYAC (1250 AM, 1965-1981) was the Central District's soul station and the primary broadcast outlet for local R&B musicians. If English's music was played on radio at all, KYAC is the most likely station. Lloyd Jones (Quincy Jones's brother) was a KYAC DJ who championed local acts. [NEEDS VERIFICATION -- KYAC playlists are not preserved in accessible archives.]

theory_nodes.concept: The Unrecorded (artist-city pattern where a musician is fully embedded in the city's musical culture -- performing in its venues, known to its community, part of its scene -- but whose documentation does not survive the transition from live performance to permanent archive; the pattern is about preservation mechanics, not artistic quality; the Unrecorded musician played, was heard, mattered to the people in the room, but the room did not have a tape recorder, or the tape degraded, or the 45 was never pressed, or the press run was so small that no copy survived; the structural foundation beneath even The Foundation -- you cannot build a scene without the musicians who perform in it but whose names are not written down)
examples: Larry English (degree 33) -- Central District R&B musician, 1960s-1970s, 'local' label, no documentation found in any searched source. The paradigmatic Unrecorded.

Contrast with The Foundation (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- Rabb was documented through his associations with Hendrix, Ballin' Jack, Santana, War. The Foundation is the structural support player whose documentation survives because of WHO he supported. The Unrecorded is the musician whose documentation does not survive because there was no famous association to anchor it.

Contrast with The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- Jones left Seattle and became the most documented musician in the engine. English stayed local and became the least documented. Same neighborhood, same musical culture, opposite documentation outcomes.

Contrast with The Incubation (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- Charles was incubated by the Central District and documented because he became famous elsewhere. English was incubated by the same neighborhood but did not leave, and the local documentation infrastructure did not preserve his work.
The unnamed musicians of any scene: every jazz club had a house band whose members are forgotten. Every church choir had soloists whose names are lost. The Unrecorded is universal.
theory_nodes.description: The Unrecorded is the thirty-fourth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes a musician who is fully embedded in the city's musical culture -- performing in its venues, known to its community, integral to its scene -- but whose documentation does not survive the transition from live performance to permanent archive. This is not a pattern about failure or insignificance. It is a pattern about the mechanics of preservation. The Central District's 1960s-1970s R&B scene produced hundreds of musicians who performed nightly in the neighborhood's clubs, sang in its churches, learned instruments in its schools and jam sessions. The overwhelming majority of these musicians were never documented in formats that later archivists could recover. They are the base of the pyramid beneath the famous names. Larry English represents this base. His degree in the engine is a named space for the unnamed -- a structural acknowledgment that the scene existed because of the musicians who constituted it, whether or not their names survive in any archive.

ancestors: The Foundation (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- the structural support player. The Unrecorded is the layer beneath the foundation: the soil the foundation rests on.
The Incubation (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- the city as incubator. The Unrecorded was incubated by the same city but did not emerge into the documented world.
The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- the city's most famous product. The Unrecorded is the city's most anonymous product. Same origin, opposite outcomes.

The Relay (degree 31, Brenda Lee Eager) -- the unseen carrier of tradition. The Unrecorded is the unseen performer of tradition. Both are invisible; the Relay is invisible in credits, the Unrecorded is invisible in archives.

Central District musical infrastructure (Jackson Street clubs, KYAC, Garfield High School, church choirs, Kearney Barton's studio) -- the ecosystem that produced both the documented and the undocumented.
genealogy.relationship: The Unrecorded pattern completes a structural trilogy: The Export (degree 0) leaves and is documented. The Foundation (degree 32) supports and is partially documented. The Unrecorded (degree 33) stays and is not documented. Three relationships to the same city, three documentation outcomes. The engine now maps the full spectrum of artist-city preservation: from exhaustive to absent.

theory_nodes.concept: The room tone (thirty-fourth acoustic element -- the ambient sound of a venue before and during a performance; the hum of the crowd, the clink of glasses, the creak of the floor, the resonance of the room itself; in audio engineering, 'room tone' is the baseline sound of a space -- what you hear when the performers are silent but the room is alive; for Larry English, the room tone IS the surviving music because without recordings, all that remains is the memory of what the room sounded like; follows the groove lock -- after the bass locks in, the room responds; the audience's presence made audible)
examples: Larry English (degree 33) -- the room tone is the only acoustic element that survives when no recording exists. The Golden Crown at midnight. The Black and Tan Club on a Saturday. The sound of the room is the sound of the community. Without recordings, the room tone is the music.

Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- 'the groove lock': the bass line holds the ensemble. The room tone is the response: the audience that the groove lock holds in place. The groove lock is the cause; the room tone is the effect.

Contrast with every prior acoustic element (degrees 0-32): all previous elements could be heard on recordings. The room tone cannot be heard on any recording of Larry English because no recording exists. The room tone is the first acoustic element defined by its absence from the archive.
The room tone of any live venue: Sun Studio's slap-back echo, CBGB's sticky floor reverb, the Golden Crown's late-night hum. Room tone is place-specific and irreproducible.
theory_nodes.description: The room tone is the thirty-fourth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. In audio engineering, room tone is the baseline ambient sound of a space -- what a microphone picks up when no one is playing but the room is occupied. It includes the crowd's murmur, the clink of glasses, the hum of amplifiers, the resonance characteristics of the walls and ceiling. Room tone is what makes a live recording sound 'live' and what is deliberately removed in studio recordings. For Larry English, the room tone takes on a different meaning: it is the ONLY surviving acoustic element. Without recordings, the specific musical content of English's performances cannot be recovered. What can be reconstructed -- from the documented accounts of Central District venues, from the Wheedle's Groove documentary, from oral histories -- is the room tone: the ambient acoustic environment of the clubs where he played. The room tone follows the groove lock (degree 32) in the engine's acoustic progression. The groove lock established the bass as immovable rhythmic foundation; the room tone is what happens around and above that foundation -- the acoustic environment that the audience creates by being present. After the groove locks, the room responds.

ancestors: The groove lock (degree 32, Luther Rabb) -- the bass locks in; the room tone is the room's response to the locked groove
The mellow undertow (degree 31, Brenda Lee Eager) -- sub-surface warmth; the room tone is the full-surface ambient environment
The rough brrit (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- eruptive. The room tone is sustained -- not an event but a condition
All 33 prior acoustic elements -- each defined by what CAN be heard on recordings. The room tone is the first element defined by what CANNOT be heard because no recording exists.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-four acoustic elements across thirty-four degrees. The room tone opens a new conceptual phase: the acoustic element as environmental condition rather than musical event. The progression groove lock (32) -> room tone (33) maps the transition from musical architecture to acoustic environment. The post-brrit reconstruction is fully complete: rough brrit (30) -> mellow undertow (31) -> groove lock (32) established a new foundation. The room tone (33) is the first element built ON that foundation -- the ambient space in which the foundation operates.

theory_nodes.concept: The documentation gap as data (the principle that the absence of documentation for a musician, genre, or scene is itself a significant finding rather than a research failure; the gap reveals structural inequities in preservation infrastructure -- who had access to recording studios, which labels signed which artists, which archives preserved which recordings, which historians interviewed which musicians; documentation gaps are not neutral; they track lines of race, class, geography, and institutional power)
examples: Larry English (degree 33) -- the paradigmatic documentation gap. No online results across 15+ sources. The gap reveals that Central District R&B musicians who did not sign major labels, did not associate with famous musicians, and did not have their 45s survive in collector markets simply disappear from the historical record.

Wheedle's Groove recovery (2001-2014) -- DJ Mr. Supreme found a Black on White Affair 45 in a 99-cent bin. The compilation exists because ONE collector found surviving vinyl. For every recovered artist, there are unknown numbers of unrecovered artists.

KYAC playlists (1965-1981) -- the radio station's playlists would document which local musicians were played and when. These playlists are not preserved in any accessible archive. The gap in KYAC documentation is a gap in the history of Central District music.

Kearney Barton's complete client list -- Barton recorded hundreds of local musicians. His full client list is not publicly available. The gap between known Barton clients and actual Barton clients contains unknown musicians.
theory_nodes.description: The documentation gap as data is a historiographic principle introduced at degree 33. It holds that the absence of documentation for a musician is not a research failure but a research finding. The gap tells us something specific about the structures of preservation: who had access to recording studios, which labels chose to sign which artists, which recordings were pressed in quantities large enough to survive, which archives chose to preserve which materials, which historians chose to interview which musicians. These decisions -- made by studio owners, label executives, pressing plant operators, archivists, and historians -- determine who enters the permanent record and who does not. The decisions are not random. They track lines of race (segregated musicians' unions in Seattle until the 1950s), class (studio time costs money), geography (Pacific Northwest isolation from national music industry centers), and institutional power (major labels vs. self-released 45s). Larry English's documentation gap is a specific instance of a general pattern: the Central District produced far more musicians than it documented, and the musicians who were not documented were disproportionately those without connections to major labels, famous associates, or physical recordings that survived in collectible condition.

ancestors: The Unrecorded (degree 33) -- the artist-city pattern that names the undocumented musician. The documentation gap as data is the theoretical framework that explains WHY the Unrecorded exists.
Genre refusal at band scale (degree 32, War) -- the gap between market label and sonic reality. The documentation gap is the gap between lived musical culture and archived musical culture.

All prior degrees with NEEDS VERIFICATION tags -- partial documentation gaps that flagged specific uncertainties. Degree 33 elevates the gap from a tag on specific claims to a structural principle about the entire scene.
genealogy.relationship: The documentation gap as data is the engine's first explicitly historiographic theory node. Prior theory nodes described musical phenomena (acoustic elements, blue note deployments, genre stages, artist-city patterns). This node describes the process by which musical phenomena enter or fail to enter the historical record. It is meta-theory: theory about the conditions of knowledge rather than about music itself.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the ghost bend (thirty-third deployment; the blue note that was played but not recorded; the micro-tonal inflection that existed in the air of a live performance but was never captured on tape or vinyl; the most fundamental form of the blue note -- spontaneous, unrepeatable, existing only for the audience in the room at the moment of performance; completes a 33-degree arc from fully documented (degree 0, Jones) to fully undocumented (degree 33, English))
examples: Larry English (degree 33) -- the ghost bend is every blue note English ever played in a Central District venue. None were recorded. All were heard. All are gone.

Luther Rabb (degree 32) -- funk bass bend: the blue note captured in War's recordings. The ghost bend is the blue note NOT captured. Rabb's bends survive on vinyl; English's bends survive only in the memory of whoever was in the room.

Quincy Jones (degree 0) -- bass-line blue note: the fully documented blue note at the engine's origin. The ghost bend at degree 33 is the fully undocumented blue note. 33-degree arc from documented to undocumented.
Every live performance ever: musicians play notes that are never captured. The ghost bend names this universal phenomenon and gives it a place in the blue note taxonomy.
theory_nodes.description: Thirty-third deployment of the blue note thread. The ghost bend is the blue note that was played but not recorded -- the micro-tonal inflection that existed in the air of a live performance but was never captured on tape or vinyl. This is the blue note in its most fundamental form: spontaneous, unrepeatable, bound to the specific moment and room and audience of its performance. Every live performance contains ghost bends -- notes that the musician plays once and that are never heard again. For most documented musicians, the ghost bends coexist with recorded bends; the listener can hear the recorded versions and infer what the live versions might have sounded like. For Larry English, there are ONLY ghost bends. No recordings survive. Every blue note he ever played is a ghost bend. The ghost bend completes a 33-degree philosophical arc in the blue note thread: from the fully documented (degree 0, Quincy Jones's bass-line blue note, preserved in studio recordings) to the fully undocumented (degree 33, Larry English's ghost bends, preserved in nothing). Thirty-three deployments have now traversed the entire spectrum of documentation: bass-line (0), guitar (1), vocal (3), trumpet-warm (4), piano crush (5), altered voicings (6), trombone slide (7), bass fundamental (8), sax voice (9), alto cool bend (10), flute bent (11), bebop chromatic (12), modal integration (13), post-genre floating (14), assertive brass (15), synthesizer pitch bend (16), vocal glide (17), soul-vocal (18), collective-soul (19), americana-soul (20), funk-brass (21), vocal-funk (22), gospel-vocal (23), dynastic-soul (24), collective-activist (25), metamorphic (26), brass-section smear (27), supper-club vocal bend (28), boogaloo guitar bend (29), gospel-soul rasp (30), duet vocal warmth (31), funk bass bend (32), ghost bend (33).

ancestors: Blue note -- all thirty-two prior deployments (degrees 0-32)
Blue note -- funk bass bend (degree 32, Luther Rabb); the last RECORDED blue note mechanism. The ghost bend is the first UNRECORDED mechanism.
Blue note -- bass-line (degree 0, Quincy Jones); the first deployment. The ghost bend at degree 33 completes the 33-degree arc from fully documented to fully undocumented.

Blue note -- gospel-soul rasp (degree 30, Ray Charles); the blue note as vocal texture. The ghost bend is the blue note as absence -- texture that was present in performance but absent from the archive.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-three deployments across thirty-four degrees. The ghost bend introduces a new category of blue note: the unrecorded. All prior deployments could be heard on recordings. The ghost bend cannot. This does not make it less real -- it makes it more ephemeral. The blue note thread now spans the full documentation spectrum: studio precision (0) to live absence (33). The arc is complete.

seattle_connection: CENTRAL DISTRICT -- explicit neighborhood designation in the CSV. This is the first degree where 'Central District' is the stated neighborhood rather than an inferred biographical connection. Larry English IS the Central District -- not connected to it through biography, association, or inference, but designated as belonging to it at the data level. SEVEN Central District degrees in the first 34 (0, 3, 4, 5, 30, 32, 33) -- the densest geographic cluster in the engine. THE UNRECORDED as 34th artist-city pattern -- the musician embedded in the scene whose documentation does not survive. THE ROOM TONE as 34th acoustic element -- the ambient sound of the venue, the only surviving acoustic element when no recordings exist. THE GHOST BEND as 33rd blue note deployment -- the blue note that was played but not recorded, completing a 33-degree arc from documented (degree 0) to undocumented (degree 33). DOCUMENTATION GAP AS DATA -- the absence of documentation is itself the finding; the gap reveals structural inequities in preservation infrastructure. E=5 SECOND CONSECUTIVE (degrees 32-33) -- reinterpreted from personal tidal energy (Rabb) to scene-cumulative tidal energy (English). Distribution 2:13:6:11:2. PURE R&B continues at Genre Stage 15 (second consecutive). LOCAL as label designation -- first unnamed label in the engine, ending the four-degree major-label streak (Prestige-Atlantic-Mercury-Mercury at degrees 29-32). GENDER: 6:28 ratio (~17.6%). Male. No change in women count.

LOCAL MUSICIANS as structural base: English as representative of the hundreds of undocumented Central District musicians. The pyramid principle.

WHEEDLE'S GROOVE BOUNDARY: The compilation series (Vols. I & II) represents the recoverable boundary of the Central District scene. Musicians below that boundary are undocumented. English is below the boundary.
